sentences.text: And Russian.
Yes.
I believe they had one, and he was a religious teacher.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had once a week, religion in the Polish school for one hour.
It was a general-- it wasn't where they taught you the Bible-- the Torah or anything like that.
It was, I think-- I don't even remember, to be correct, what they were teaching in-- in that class, yeah.
Came in, we had the regular classes in Yiddish.
Not Hebrew-- Yiddish.
I believe many of those children-- the Polish and the Ukrainian-- dropped out.
They went-- they went to different schools.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The attitude was that it's now-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's not their country.
And I would say that they believed that the Jews were running this-- the-- actually, the predominance of the population in town was Jewish.
Yes.
And as I understand it, sometimes in the 1800s, we even had a Jewish mayor-- when the Austrians were there.
But the-- what I failed to tell you is the Ukrainian population was just as anti-Semitic as the Polish population.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, we lived in a Jewish area, and all the villages, all the towns, within miles, within kilometers-- 50 kilometers or so-- around the city were all Ukrainian.
They-- it was actually part of the Western Ukraine, 100 years before, or so.
The population was predominantly Ukrainian.
Even during the Austro-Hungarian Empire, yes.
Yeah.
Yes, Ukrainian, Polish, because the Polish government was in charge.
And they ran things.
And-- and the Jews were being abused by everybody.
[LAUGHS]
We spoke Polish, yes.
Yes, well, as a youngster, yes.
Yes.
Yiddish.
Well, Mother was a-- I believe, had a lot of schooling in German in Austria, so she was well-versed in German.
And she-- we used to have German books at home.
But when we were born, it was Poland, so we didn't take up German.
But I knew how to read, at that time-- I still do.
I could read German-- the modern German, not the old--
Different script, yes.
Well, I didn't learn Russian until the-- the Russians came-- which was-- it's very similar to the Ukrainian.

But it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as the war started in '41, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I was part of the Soviet Armed Forces, all we spoke was Russian, because most of the men were from different areas of the Soviet Union.
Not only on the street, but with associating with all the men.

They started, yes, I believe in 1939 to '41, they started teaching us Russian, which was also-- but the main language was actually Ukrainian, because they considered that part of the Western Ukraine-- [SPEAKING UKRAINIAN].
He was the main teacher, the main-- like my father, because I had no father.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I was about three or four years old, he used to bounce me on his knee, and sing to me the [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
[CRYING]
Well, he used to get up every morning, about 5 o'clock and go to shul.
And many times, he used to take me along-- dress me in the morning, feed me, whatever there was, and take me along.
Excuse me, I've got to wipe my eyes.
Yes, it was about three blocks away.
And it was an old-fashioned [? stubula, ?] and it was Orthodox-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say extremely Orthodox.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after the services, he used to take me home.
He used to go evening services, also.
Sometimes I went with him, sometimes I did not.
Yes, eh-- when he came home, he was reading from his books-- and Russia.
I went to cheder, and I learned Hebrew.
But the way they taught us is how to read, but they never explained what it meant.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I can read the prayers fluently, [LAUGHS] but many times, I don't know what I'm reading about.
[LAUGHS] It was a-- a general religious life they had in the old country.
Yes, Grandma kept kosher, Mother and Grandma kept kosher.
All Jewish holidays were celebrated.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They-- whatever they were able to afford-- they used the buy matzos and-- and chicken.
And the Ukrainians used to pass in front of the house-- coming from the villages, you know-- with their horse and wagons, and they used to stop before getting to the market.
And Mother used to buy eggs and potatoes, [LAUGHS] by the sack.
And it lasted, because the larger the quantity the cheaper it was.
You know.
[LAUGHS]
Actually, where we lived, there was not.
It was just a family thing.
Because most people were poor in that area, and they couldn't afford to feed anybody else except they-- hardly  had enough to feed their own children.
There were some well-to-do people who lived that way, who had everything.
But not where we lived-- not our family.
Yes.
Like I said, in 1938, we got the electricity, and my mother went out and bought our small radio.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we used to listen to broadcasts from different countries, because where we lived we were very close to Romania.
We were very close to Hungary.
We were very close to Germany.
We were very close to the Soviet Union-- we were right on the border, practically.
And when you had a small radio, you could hear practically every country that was broadcasting in Europe.
Even London had a broadcast in Polish.
So we had an idea.
And also, in 1937 or 1938, we suddenly had a visitor at home.
My grandfather had a sister who moved to Berlin in the early '20s, and they had a daughter who was born in Berlin.
And they lived a wonderful life-- beautiful-- very well-educated, with centralized plumbing, and electricity, and theaters, and everything you could wish for, like you have in this country.
I'm not sure which year, 1937 or '38, she was thrown out of Germany, because she was considered a Polish citizen, because her parents were Polish.
Her parents died in Germany.
They were buried in Germany.
Before the war.
So considering the fact that she had nowhere to go, except her first cousin-- which was my mother-- and her sister Pepi, she came to live with us.
Just imagine the difference in life.

It's like taking a girl, a young woman, who was born in New York City, or in Washington DC, or in San Francisco, and throwing in to live in Iraq, or Iran, in a Third World country, or in Tijuana, Mexico.
She died with my parent-- with my mother in World War II.
Well, the discussions were many times between my mother and her.
Grandparents were old-- actually, Grandfather wasn't there anymore.
He died in 1933.
Grandma used to sit and listen.
They spoke German most of the time.

And she explained what was going on in Germany, with the concentration camps, with the abuses, with the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] store-- Jewish store keepers where they had signs saying, don't buy Jewish.
And my mother remembered a different type of Germany from World War I, and she couldn't believe it.
She used to say, no, the German people are kind.
The Austrians are kind.
They will not kill women and children.
They will kill a soldier in a war if you fight them, but they will not harm the general Jewish population.
Nobody believed it.
Very few people believed it-- they were afraid of the Russian cossacks-- they didn't want to go to Russia, to Siberia.

Because the Russians-- the Soviet Union-- well, they didn't live in the Soviet Union, but they still remember the cossacks from World War I, when they came into our hometown, Kolomyja, and the first thing they did is cut heads off the Jews with their sabers.
Memories like this, you don't forget.
They didn't want to leave what?
Where would they go?
Nobody wanted them.
My older brother left, in 1939, Kolomyja, and went to the Lithuanian border and crossed it with a group of young men going to Palestine.
He was arrested by the Lithuanians and sent back the Poland.
They never made it.
So he perished.
As much as we could understand in those days.
We--
Oh, yes.
[LAUGHS] We were bombed that day.
[LAUGHS]
Well, yeah, there was a military compound, and we could hear bombs falling.
It was the Polish 49th Division-- [POLISH] means, what is it called?
Infantry.
But within a day, the Russians came in, and that was the end of the war in 1939.
For Kolomyja, yeah.
I was in the central part of the city.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before the Russians came in, that day before, there was looting because the Poles were leaving to the Romanian border.
I could actually see Polish army units-- we were about 40-- 30 or 40 kilometers from the Romanian border, at that time-- and Polish army units were retreating, running away from the Germans.
But they didn't know they were gonna get captured by the Russians.
Yeah, that's OK.
Do I what?
Well, you knew that they were going-- we thought that they were going to the front lines.
But actually, we realized that this was part of the retreat-- they were trying to get-- Romania was-- to get to Romania to escape from both of them, I guess.
Yeah, then the Russians came in the next day.
And the first thing that came in was Russian tanks-- T-34s which we never saw.
Polish army didn't have tanks like that-- they were huge in those-- for those days.

And as they were coming in, they were greeted by the population-- not by the Polish population, not by-- I don't think by the Ukrainian population, but the Jews felt that they were being liberated from oppression.
Yes.
When I saw what?
No, no, it was mostly government property.
Like, for instance, there was a cigarette warehouse, and cigarettes were government-controlled in Poland.
It was a monopoly, like a government monopoly.
So everybody was looting cigarettes.
[LAUGHS] They were looting the stores or food that belonged to the Polish army or whatever Polish-- because the army was gone, the Polish police was gone.
There was no authority.
And for that day, or day and a half, civilians picked up guns, and they're trying to control, to hold crime down.
To protect the general population-- I would say the Jewish population-- from pogroms.
I remember a Ukrainian peasant came into the middle of the city, riding a officer-- Polish officer's horse, and he was dragging the Polish officers behind him-- all tied up and beaten to a pulp.
Because they hated each other-- the Poles and the Ukrainians never got along.
The Poles were as bad to the Ukrainians as they were to the Jews.
And the Ukrainians were as bad to the Jews as the Poles were to the Jews-- exactly what's going on in Croatia.
You know, the Muslims are fighting the Catholics, the Catholics are fighting the-- what do you call it?
Not the Protestants, the Pravoslavnis, which is Russian Orthodox.
We had the same situation.
Positively.
Well, they-- they picked up some guns-- I don't know where the guns came from-- rifles.
And there was a militia right away, a local militia, which was this barge right after the Russians came, within a day or so.
And we did have local Communists who-- Jewish Communists-- who became the rulers of the town.
They worked in cahoots with the Russian-- new Russian military government that came in.
They did nothing.
They eliminated private stores-- the well-to-do Jews, their stores were confiscated.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Many of them, apparently, when known that they were anti-Communists, were sent to Siberia.
What happened to them?
I have no idea.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Religion was frowned upon.
Like I said, they had Yiddish in the schools, but not religion.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They started closing some of the synagogues.
And that was it.
Yes, yes.
We received a passport-- a Russian Soviet passport-- saying that we are Soviet citizen.
And it said we're [RUSSIAN], which means Hebrew.
And I believe we were the only ones of all the Soviet nationalities that had on their Russian passport-- that was an internal passport, not an external-- that it said the religion of the Jew.
Nobody else had that on the-- on the--
Some of the were, yes.
Yes.
Well, I don't think I ran into anti-Semitism before 1941, as a civilian.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We thought that some of them might have been anti-Semitic, but this was very hard to pinpoint exactly what-- what transpired.
Many of the Jews were arrested because, you know, the only way to make a living in Poland, and here the Russians came in, is by trading-- you buy something, you sell something.
They called it black marketeering.
There were no jobs to be available for the general population when the Russians came in.
There were a few government jobs, but the only way you could, for the first year or two, to-- to make a living is to continue what you did before, you know.
That was considered black marketeering.
So many of them were arrested, thrown in jail.
To us, it was anti-Semitism.
To them, it was black marketeering.
They called it a different name.
For the better, we were still the same, except we couldn't to receive the packages and the money from the United States.
It was tougher.
Mother became an official teacher for a kindergarten class, and was paid some rubles by the government, and that's how we survived.
Because she was running this-- this kindergarten-like type of school-- it wasn't hers anymore.
It was a town-run school, and she became a teacher, which was paid a few rubles by-- by whatever.
I don't remember how many or what.
Sure.
